The **Steyr AUG** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Armee-Universal-Gewehr*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'army universal rifle') is an [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") [bullpup](/wiki/Bullpup "Bullpup") [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle") chambered for the [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO") [intermediate cartridge](/wiki/Intermediate_cartridge "Intermediate cartridge"), designed in the 1960s by [Steyr-Daimler-Puch](/wiki/Steyr-Daimler-Puch "Steyr-Daimler-Puch"), and now manufactured by [Steyr Arms](/wiki/Steyr_Arms "Steyr Arms") GmbH & Co KG.

The AUG was adopted by the [Austrian Army](/wiki/Austrian_Federal_Army "Austrian Federal Army") in 1977 as the **StG 77** (*Sturmgewehr 77*),[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-6) where it replaced the [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO") [StG 58](/wiki/Sturmgewehr_58 "Sturmgewehr 58") [automatic rifle](/wiki/Automatic_rifle "Automatic rifle").[[7]](#cite_note-ezell_223-7) In production since 1977, it is the standard small arm of the *[Bundesheer](/wiki/Bundesheer "Bundesheer")* and various [Austrian federal police](/wiki/Federal_Police_(Austria) "Federal Police (Austria)") units and its variants have also been adopted by the armed forces of dozens of countries, with some using it as a standard-issue [service rifle](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle").

The importation of the Steyr AUG into the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") began in the 1980s as the AUG/SA (SA denoting semi-automatic). The AUG was banned from importation in 1989 under [President George H. W. Bush](/wiki/President_George_H._W._Bush "President George H. W. Bush")'s [executive order](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") restricting the import of foreign-made semiautomatic rifles deemed not to have "a legitimate sporting use." Six years into the ban, AUG buyers gained a reprieve as cosmetic changes to the carbine's design allowed importation once again. Changes included redesigning its [pistol grip](/wiki/Pistol_grip "Pistol grip") into a thumbhole stock and leaving its barrel [unthreaded](/wiki/Threading_(manufacturing) "Threading (manufacturing)") to prevent attachment of a [flash hider](/wiki/Flash_hider "Flash hider") or [suppressor](/wiki/Suppressor "Suppressor").

The [Federal Assault Weapons Ban](/wiki/Federal_Assault_Weapons_Ban "Federal Assault Weapons Ban"), passed in 1994, further prohibited the manufacture of additional Steyr AUGs or their copies. The ban expired in 2004, and in 2008, [Steyr Arms](/wiki/Steyr_Arms "Steyr Arms") worked with [Sabre Defence](/wiki/Sabre_Defence "Sabre Defence") to produce parts legally in the U.S.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Design details

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design details")]

The Steyr AUG is a [selective-fire](/wiki/Selective-fire "Selective-fire"), [bullpup](/wiki/Bullpup "Bullpup") assault rifle with a conventional [gas-piston-operated](/wiki/Gas-operated_reloading "Gas-operated reloading") action that fires from a [closed bolt](/wiki/Closed_bolt "Closed bolt").[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) It is designed as a [Modular Weapon System](/wiki/Modular_Weapon_System "Modular Weapon System") that could be quickly configured as an [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle"), a [carbine](/wiki/Carbine "Carbine"), a [submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun") and even an [open-bolt](/wiki/Open-bolt "Open-bolt") [light machine gun](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun").

The AUG is chambered for the [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO") cartridge and has the standard 1:9 [rifling](/wiki/Rifling "Rifling") twist that will stabilise both SS109/M855 and M193 rounds. Some nations, including Australia, Ireland and New Zealand, use a version with a 1:7 twist optimised for the SS109 NATO round. The submachine gun variants are chambered in either [9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum") or [.40 S&W](/wiki/.40_S%26W ".40 S&W"). In Enforce Tac 2025, a Steyr AUG chambered in 7.62×39mm was unveiled.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The AUG consists of six interchangeable assemblies: the [barrel](/wiki/Barrel_(firearms) "Barrel (firearms)"), [receiver](/wiki/Receiver_(firearms) "Receiver (firearms)") with integrated [telescopic sight](/wiki/Telescopic_sight "Telescopic sight") or [Picatinny rail](/wiki/Picatinny_rail "Picatinny rail"), [bolt carrier assembly](/wiki/Bolt_(firearm) "Bolt (firearm)"), [trigger mechanism](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms) "Trigger (firearms)"), [stock](/wiki/Stock_(firearms) "Stock (firearms)"), and [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms) "Magazine (firearms)").[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) The AUG employs advanced firearms technology, and is constructed from multiple [polymers](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") and [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") components.

The AUG comes with a muzzle cap, spare bolt for left-handed shooters, blank-firing adaptor, cleaning kit, sling and either an American [M7](/wiki/M7_bayonet "M7 bayonet") or German KCB-77 M1 bayonet.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Engineering")]

The quick-change barrel used in the AUG is cold hammer-forged for increased precision and durability; its bore, chamber and certain components of the gas system are [chrome-plated](/wiki/Chrome_plating "Chrome plating") (currently nitride on US market rifles). The standard rifle-length barrel features 6 right-hand grooves and a rifling twist rate of 228 mm (1:9 in). An external sleeve is shrunk onto the barrel and carries the gas port and cylinder, gas valve and forward grip hinge jaw. There is a short cylinder which contains a piston and its associated return spring. The barrel locks into a steel insert inside the receiver through a system of eight lugs arranged around the chamber end, and is equipped with a folding vertical grip that helps to pivot and withdraw the barrel during barrel changes. The most compact of the barrels has a fixed vertical grip.

The receiver housing is a steel-reinforced aluminium extrusion finished with a baked enamel coating.[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) It holds the steel bearings for the barrel lugs and the guide rods. The non-reciprocating plastic cocking handle works in a slot on the left side of the receiver and is connected to the bolt carrier's left guide rod. The cocking handle has a [forward assist](/wiki/Forward_assist "Forward assist") feature—alternatively called a "silent cocking device"—allowing the user to fully push the bolt home without racking the charging handle.[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10)[[12]](#cite_note-Choat-12) A bolt hold-open device locks the bolt carrier back after the last round has been fired.[[12]](#cite_note-Choat-12) The AUG A3s feature a bolt release button; before this development, all AUGs and the USR required the user to rack the charging handle to disengage the bolt hold-open after inserting a fresh magazine. Older versions of the AUG can be upgraded to use the newer A3 stock and hammer pack.

The AUG's stock is made from fibreglass-reinforced [polyamide 66](/wiki/Nylon_66 "Nylon 66"). At the forward end is the pistol grip with an enlarged forward trigger guard completely enclosing the firing hand that allows the rifle to be operated with winter gloves.[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) The trigger is hung permanently on the pistol grip, together with its two operating rods, which run in guides past the magazine housing. Behind that is the locking catch for the stock group. Pressing this to the right will separate the receiver and stock. The magazine catch is behind the housing, on the underside of the stock. Above the housing are the two ejector openings, one of which is always covered by a removable strip of plastic. The rear of the stock forms the actual shoulder rest, which contains the hammer unit and the end of the bolt path. The butt is closed by an endplate which is held in place by the rear sling swivel. This swivel is attached to a pin which pushes in across the butt and secures the plate. There is a cavity under the buttplate that holds a cleaning kit.

### Operating mechanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Operating mechanism")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Steyr_AUG "Special:EditPage/Steyr AUG") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(February 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The AUG has a [rotating bolt](/wiki/Rotating_bolt "Rotating bolt") that features 7 radial locking lugs and is unlocked through a pin on the bolt body and a recessed camming guide machined into the bolt carrier.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The bolt carrier itself is guided by two guide rods brazed to it and these rods run inside steel bearings in the receiver. The guide rods are hollow and contain the return springs. The bolt also contains a claw extractor that forms the eighth locking lug and a spring-loaded "bump"-type casing ejector.

The gas cylinder is offset to the right side of the barrel and works with one of the two guide rods. The AUG uses a short-stroke piston system where the right guide rod serves as the action rod, transmitting the rearward motion of the gas-driven piston to the bolt carrier. The left-hand rod provides retracting handle pressure when connected by the forward assist and can also be utilised as a reamer to remove fouling in the gas cylinder. The firearm uses a 3-position gas valve. The first setting, marked with a small dot, is used for normal operation. The second setting, illustrated with a large dot, indicates fouled conditions. The third, "GR" closed position is used to launch [rifle grenades](/wiki/Rifle_grenade "Rifle grenade") (of the non-bullet trap type).

The AUG is hammer-fired and the firing mechanism is contained in the rear of the stock, near the butt, covered by a synthetic rubber shoulder plate. The hammer group is made entirely of plastics except for the springs and pins and is contained in an open-topped plastic box which lies between the magazine and the buttplate. During firing the recoiling bolt group travels over the top of it, resetting the hammer. Since the trigger is located some distance away, it transmits its energy through a sear lever which passes by the side of the magazine. The firing pin is operated by a polymer hammer under pressure from a coil spring.

Some common criticisms of the AUG's trigger are the trigger feeling "mushy" and having excessive take-up. This is due to the relatively imprecise nature of how the trigger linkage is installed by the factory. The trigger can be "tuned" by the user in roughly 15 to 60 minutes with a 12 inch-pound wrench with a hex bit.

### Firing mechanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Firing mechanism")]

The AUG's firing mechanism can also be changed at will, into a variety of configurations, including semi-auto and full-auto, semi-auto and three-round-burst, semi-auto-only, or any other combination that the user desires.[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-14) It can also be converted into an open-bolt full-auto-only mode of fire, which allows for improved cooling and eliminates [cook off](/wiki/Cook_off "Cook off") problems when the AUG is used as a light machine gun.[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-14)

### Trigger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Trigger")]

The AUG features a [progressive trigger](/wiki/Progressive_trigger "Progressive trigger") (pulling the trigger halfway produces [semi-automatic fire](/wiki/Semi-automatic_fire "Semi-automatic fire"), pulling the trigger all the way to the rear produces [fully automatic fire](/wiki/Fully_automatic_fire "Fully automatic fire")), and a safety mechanism (cross-bolt, button type) located immediately above the hand grip.[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) In its "safe" position (white dot), the trigger is mechanically disabled; pressing the safety button to the left exposes a red dot and indicates the rifle is ready to fire. Some versions have an ALO or "automatic lockout", a small projection at the base of the trigger. This was first included on the Irish Defence Forces variant of the rifle, and soon after, the Australian Defence Forces variant. In the exposed position, the ALO stops the trigger being squeezed past the semi-automatic position. If needed, the ALO can be pushed up to permit automatic fire.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Ammunition and magazine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ammunition and magazine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/30_Schuss_.223_Remington_f%C3%BCr_STG77_-_AssE3.jpg/250px-30_Schuss_.223_Remington_f%C3%BCr_STG77_-_AssE3.jpg)](/wiki/File:30_Schuss_.223_Remington_f%C3%BCr_STG77_-_AssE3.jpg)

Steyr AUG with a loaded 30-round proprietary magazine

The AUG is fed from a detachable proprietary translucent-polymer double-column box magazine with either a 30- or 42-round capacity.[[16]](#cite_note-steyr-aug-catalogue-16) Optional NATO stock for STANAG magazine compatibility is also available.[[4]](#cite_note-aug-a1-a2-4)[[16]](#cite_note-steyr-aug-catalogue-16)

### Receivers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Receivers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Interpolitex_2011_%28405-9%29.jpg/250px-Interpolitex_2011_%28405-9%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interpolitex_2011_(405-9).jpg)

Steyr AUGs with tan and black finish. Note the different type of Picatinny rail upper receivers

The AUG's receiver can be changed from the standard model with a carrying handle and built-in 1.5× optical sight[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-14) to the 'Special Receiver' which has a STANAG [scope mount](/wiki/Scope_mount "Scope mount") to allow for the use of a variety of scopes and sights.[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-14) In later models (A2 and A3), it has several different types of receivers with Picatinny rails.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Scope_StG_77.JPG/250px-Scope_StG_77.JPG)](/wiki/File:Scope_StG_77.JPG)

Steyr AUG's telescopic sight. Note the backup iron sights on top of the scope

The AUG has a 1.5× telescopic sight that is integrated with the receiver casting and is made by [Swarovski Optik](/wiki/Swarovski_Optik "Swarovski Optik"). It contains a simple black ring [reticle](/wiki/Reticle "Reticle"). The sight cannot be set to a specific range but can be adjusted for windage and elevation for an initial zero and is designed to be calibrated for 300 m. It also has a backup iron sight with a rear notch and front blade, cast into the top of the aluminium optical sight housing, in case of failure or damage to the primary optical sight. The sight is also equipped with a set of three illuminated dots (one on the front blade and two at the rear) for use in low-level lighting conditions. In order to mount a wide range of optics and accessories, a receiver with a [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")-standard Picatinny rail and detachable carrying handle was also developed and introduced in December 1997.[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) Modern AUGs are equipped with, or can have the Picatinny rail swapped out with, an A3SF 60mm height ×3 optic with optional riser and additional crosshair within the "donut" black ring. This specific optic can be piggybacked with other optics on top, as is the norm with Austrian special forces, due to the Picatinny rail included on top of the optic.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Barrels and muzzle devices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Barrels and muzzle devices")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Steyr_AUG_A1_and_AG-C.jpg/250px-Steyr_AUG_A1_and_AG-C.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steyr_AUG_A1_and_AG-C.jpg)

Steyr AUG A1 with a 40 mm AG36 grenade launcher

The AUG features quick detachable barrels and are available in different lengths; including a 382 mm (15 in) compact length, 417 mm (16.4 in) carbine length and 508 mm (20 in) standard rifle-length. The muzzle device primarily used for these barrel lengths is a three-pronged, open-type flash suppressor. The flash suppressors are screwed to the muzzle and internally threaded to take a [blank-firing attachment](/wiki/Blank-firing_adaptor "Blank-firing adaptor"). AUGs equipped with the 508 mm (20 in) pattern barrels produced for military purposes are also equipped with bayonet lugs. The 417 mm (16.4 in) and 508 mm (20 in) barrels are capable of launching NATO STANAG type 22 mm rifle grenades from their integral flash hiders without the use of an adapter. AUG barrels can also mount [40 mm](/wiki/40_mm "40 mm") [M203](/wiki/M203_grenade_launcher "M203 grenade launcher") or [AG36](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_AG36 "Heckler & Koch AG36") [grenade launchers](/wiki/Grenade_launcher "Grenade launcher"). Steyr also offers 508 mm (20 in) barrel configurations fitted with a fixed, post front-sight used on the rifle version with aperture [iron sights](/wiki/Iron_sight "Iron sight").[[10]](#cite_note-ezell_224-10) A 621 mm (24.4 in) heavy barrel with an integrated lightweight folding [bipod](/wiki/Bipod "Bipod") with a closed-type ported muzzle device (combination of [flash suppressor](/wiki/Flash_suppressor "Flash suppressor") and compensator) is also available, primarily used on the AUG HBAR.[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-14)

### Stock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Stock")]

While the AUG is not fully ambidextrous, it can be configured to be used by left- or right-handed operators by changing the bolt to one that has the extractor and ejector on the appropriate side and moving the blanking plate to cover the ejection port not in use. However, there exists also a right-hand-only stock that allows for the use of [STANAG magazines](/wiki/STANAG_magazine "STANAG magazine").[[19]](#cite_note-steyr-aug.com-19)[[20]](#cite_note-auto-20)

Until March 19th, 2025, the NATO stock did not have a bolt release next to the magazine. To release the bolt after emptying the magazine, the user would need to remove the empty magazine, insert a fresh magazine, pull the charging handle back and release.

With the updated NATO stock, the operator can release the bolt using the bolt release next to the magazine well.

The Steyr AUG can also be fitted with either an [M203](/wiki/M203_grenade_launcher "M203 grenade launcher") or [AG-C](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_AG-C/EGLM "Heckler & Koch AG-C/EGLM") [grenade launcher](/wiki/Grenade_launcher "Grenade launcher").

* The **Steyr AUG A1** is fitted with an integral 1.5× optic and is available with a choice of olive or black furniture.[[4]](#cite_note-aug-a1-a2-4)
* The **Steyr AUG A2**, introduced in December 1997, features a redesigned charging handle and a detachable telescopic sight which can be replaced with a [Picatinny rail](/wiki/Picatinny_rail "Picatinny rail"). Its modularity allows a 24.4-inch barrel to be used, and the folding grip can be replaced with a Picatinny rail section, to which a bipod can be installed.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* The **Steyr AUG A3** features a Picatinny rail on top of the receiver and an external bolt release.[[22]](#cite_note-A3-22) In 2019, Steyr Arms introduced a [.300 AAC Blackout](/wiki/.300_AAC_Blackout ".300 AAC Blackout") variant of the AUG A3.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-:4-24)
  + The **Steyr AUG A3 SF** features a Picatinny rail mounted on the telescopic sight and on the right side of the receiver, and includes an external bolt release.[[25]](#cite_note-A3SF-25) The integrated telescopic sight is offered in 1.5× or 3× magnification.
  + The **Steyr AUG A3-CQC** was a cancelled prototype development of the AUG A3 and was first displayed by Steyr at the SHOT Show in 2006 and 2007. It differs in having a railed handguard attached ahead of the receiver and features an 18 in (457 mm) barrel. Because this extra railed section needed to be removed to strip the rifle for cleaning, the left side featured a quick detach lever. Due to the concerns over the extra cost and weight, along with potential issues with the reliability and consistency of the detachable handguard, the prototypes received little interest and were last seen promoted by Steyr in 2008.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 2012, the American company PJA obtained the five original prototypes from Steyr and reverse engineered them in order to produce a US-made AUG A3-CQC and conversion kits.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### AUG HBAR

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: AUG HBAR")]

The **Steyr AUG HBAR** (Heavy Barrelled Automatic Rifle), also known as the AUG LMG ([light machine gun](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun")), is essentially an [squad automatic weapon](/wiki/Squad_automatic_weapon "Squad automatic weapon") variant of the AUG. It features a heavier and longer 621 mm (24.4 in) barrel with an integrated bipod, and the standard AUG receiver with 1.5× magnification scope. It fires from an [open bolt](/wiki/Open_bolt "Open bolt") to be more suitable for sustained fire, mitigating accidental cook offs. To accomplish this, it uses a modified bolt carrier, striker and trigger mechanism with sear.[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-14)

* The **Steyr AUG HBAR-T** (Heavy Barrelled Automatic Rifle-Telescope) is similar to the AUG HBAR, but features a special receiver with a STANAG scope mount system usually fitted with a Schmidt & Bender 4×25 or Kahles ZF69 6×42 optical sight.

### AUG 9mm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: AUG 9mm")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/AUG_9mm_03.jpg/250px-AUG_9mm_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUG_9mm_03.jpg)

Steyr AUG 9mm with 420 mm (16.5 in) barrel

The **Steyr AUG 9mm**, also known as the AUG Para, is a [submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun") variant of the AUG chambered for the [9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum") cartridge and has been produced since 1988.[[1]](#cite_note-hogg2002-1) It differs from the rifle variants by having a unique 420 mm (16.5 in) barrel with six right-hand grooves at a 250 mm (1:9.8 in) rifling twist rate, with a recoil compensator, a slightly different charging handle, and a magazine well adapter enabling the use of [Steyr MPi 69](/wiki/Steyr_MPi_69 "Steyr MPi 69") 25- and 32-round box magazines. It is [blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(arms) "Blowback (arms)")-operated and fires from a closed bolt, omitting the original rifle's gas system.[[28]](#cite_note-9mm-28)[[29]](#cite_note-REMTEK_9mm-29) A conversion kit used to transform any assault rifle configuration into the submachine gun configuration is also available. The conversion kit consists of a barrel, bolt, adapter insert, and magazine.

* The **Steyr AUG A3 9mm XS** is a 9×19mm variant of the AUG A3. It fires at a cyclic rate of around 650–720 rounds per minute. It is available in either a 325 mm (12.8 in),[[30]](#cite_note-30) 350 mm (13.8 in), 365 mm (14.4 in), 420 mm (16.5 in) barrel lengths, and features a Picatinny rail system similar to the AUG A3.[[16]](#cite_note-steyr-aug-catalogue-16)
* The **Steyr AUG 40** is a [.40 S&W](/wiki/.40_S%26W ".40 S&W") variant of the AUG A3 9mm XS that uses [Glock](/wiki/Glock "Glock")-compatible double-stack .40 S&W magazines. It is offered with a 332 mm (13.1 in) barrel.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-aug-40-32)

The **StG 77** (Sturmgewehr 77) is the designation given by the [Austrian Armed Forces](/wiki/Austrian_Armed_Forces "Austrian Armed Forces") when they adopted the Steyr AUG in 1977.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

* The **StG 77 A2 Kommando** is the designation given by the Austrian Special Forces ([Jagdkommando](/wiki/Jagdkommando "Jagdkommando")) for the Steyr AUG A3 SF when it was adopted in late 2007.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

* The **StG 77 A1 MP** is the Austrian Military Police's designation for the StG 77. The rifles differ from the StG 77 by having a Picatinny rail for an [Aimpoint](/wiki/Aimpoint "Aimpoint") Micro T1 and [red dot magnifier](/wiki/Red_dot_magnifier "Red dot magnifier"), a [flash hider](/wiki/Flash_suppressor "Flash suppressor") from Ase-Utra, and [Rheinmetall](/wiki/Rheinmetall "Rheinmetall") Vario Ray [laser and light module](/wiki/Laser_sight_(firearms) "Laser sight (firearms)") mounted on the right side. Adopted in 2018.
* The **StG 77 A1 MOD** is the Austrian Army's designation of a further modified StG 77. A total of 14,400 rifles will be issued to both the militia and cadre presence units.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* The **StG 77 A2 KDO MOD** is a modified StG 77 A2 Kommando with M-LOK handguard, Kawatec trigger, Aimpoint on a riser, Steyr brass deflector and Rheinmetall light/laser module.
* The **StG 77 A1 MOD "Nightfighter"** a modified StG 77 A1 MOD with a top-mounted Aimpoint Micro T2 optic and lower-mounted telescopic sight with 3x magnification sight, a tactical laser light module that attaches to a Picatinny rail on the right side of the rifle and the rifle features a special surface coating that makes it more difficult for enemy night vision devices to detect.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 1985, the ADF ordered 67,000 F88 Austeyrs that were manufactured by Australian Defence Industries (now Thales Australia) at their [Lithgow Small Arms Factory](/wiki/Lithgow_Small_Arms_Factory "Lithgow Small Arms Factory") under licence from Steyr Mannlicher AG.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201915-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

* The **F88 Austeyr** was the standard-issue rifle that had a barrel length of 508 mm (20 in).[[45]](#cite_note-ArmyNews1988-45)
* The **F88C** **Austeyr** was the [carbine](/wiki/Carbine "Carbine") variant of the F88 Austeyr that featured a shorter 407 mm (16 in) barrel and was without a bayonet lug.[[45]](#cite_note-ArmyNews1988-45)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201928-46) The F88C was issued to armoured, helicopter and [parachute](/wiki/3rd_Battalion,_Royal_Australian_Regiment "3rd Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment") units.[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* The **F88T** **Austeyr** is a [.22 Long Rifle](/wiki/.22_Long_Rifle ".22 Long Rifle") training rifle that entered service in 1999.[[48]](#cite_note-ADI-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) The F88T was issued to infantry units, training units and to the [Australian Army Cadets](/wiki/Australian_Army_Cadets "Australian Army Cadets").[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* The **F88S** (Special) **Austeyr** was a variant of the F88 Austeyr that entered service in 1993 with an Accuracy International Mounting System (AIMS) to allow the attachment of a different sighting device.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201928-46)
* The **F88SA1 Austeyr** was an upgrade of the F88 Austeyr that entered service in 2003. The F88SA1 had an integrated [Picatinny rail](/wiki/Picatinny_rail "Picatinny rail") in place of the standard optical sight.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201936–37-52) The rail enabled the fitting of the Elcan [Wildcat sight](/wiki/C79_optical_sight "C79 optical sight"), an [AN/PVS-4](/wiki/AN/PVS-4 "AN/PVS-4") night vision sight and a night aiming device.[[53]](#cite_note-ArmyNewsApril1999-53)[[54]](#cite_note-DMO2014-54) The F88S was withdrawn from service.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201936-55)
* The **F88SA1C** **Austeyr** was the carbine variant of the F88SA1 Austeyr that had a barrel length of 407 mm (16 in).[[54]](#cite_note-DMO2014-54)
* The **F88SA2** **Austeyr** was an upgrade of the F88 Austeyr that entered service in 2009, issued to units serving in the [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_the_War_in_Afghanistan "Military history of Australia during the War in Afghanistan"). It was withdrawn due to issues and re-entered service in the end of 2010.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201950–53-56) The rifle had a two-tone colour with a "dark khaki undercarriage and a light brown upper" to match the [Disruptive Pattern Desert Uniform](/wiki/Disruptive_Pattern_Camouflage_Uniform "Disruptive Pattern Camouflage Uniform").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201951-57) Design improvements included a modified gas system for increased reliability, an enlarged ejection port, a longer Picatinny Rail on top of the rifle, a modified sight housing and a side rail mount for a torch and Night Aiming Device (NAD).[[58]](#cite_note-dtic.mil-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) The F1A1 ammunition was improved to suit the F88SA2.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201953-60)[[58]](#cite_note-dtic.mil-58) The rifle could be fitted with a standard 1.5× sight or the Trijicon [Advanced Combat Optical Gunsight](/wiki/Advanced_Combat_Optical_Gunsight "Advanced Combat Optical Gunsight") (ACOG).[[61]](#cite_note-61)

F88 Austeyrs that were fitted with the [M203 grenade launcher](/wiki/M203_grenade_launcher "M203 grenade launcher") from the M16A1 had a barrel length of 620 mm (24.4 in).[[45]](#cite_note-ArmyNews1988-45)[[62]](#cite_note-ArmyNews2001-62) In 2001, the Grenade Launcher Attachment (GLA) replaced the M203 from the M16A1 and also the [M79 grenade launcher](/wiki/M79_grenade_launcher "M79 grenade launcher").[[62]](#cite_note-ArmyNews2001-62)[[63]](#cite_note-ArmyNews2000-63) The ADF ordered 3167 GLAs.[[63]](#cite_note-ArmyNews2000-63) The GLA featured an Inter-bar (armourer attached) interface, a RM Equipment M203PI grenade launcher, and a [Knight's Armament](/wiki/Knight%27s_Armament_Company "Knight's Armament Company") quadrant sight assembly to which a Firepoint [red dot sight](/wiki/Red_dot_sight "Red dot sight") was attached.[[62]](#cite_note-ArmyNews2001-62)[[63]](#cite_note-ArmyNews2000-63) The bayonet lug and forward vertical grip were removed to fit the Inter-bar.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201936-55)

The [Advanced Individual Combat Weapon](/wiki/Advanced_Individual_Combat_Weapon "Advanced Individual Combat Weapon") (AICW) developed by the [Defence Science and Technology Organisation](/wiki/Defence_Science_and_Technology_Group "Defence Science and Technology Group"), Tenix Defence Systems, ADI, NICO and [Metal Storm](/wiki/Metal_Storm "Metal Storm") was an experimental F88 Austeyr that incorporated a top barrel for Metal Storm 30 mm rounds.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### EF88 Austeyr

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: EF88 Austeyr")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/EF88_Austeyr_rifles_during_RIMPAC_2022.jpg/250px-EF88_Austeyr_rifles_during_RIMPAC_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:EF88_Austeyr_rifles_during_RIMPAC_2022.jpg)

EF88 Austeyr displayed during RIMPAC in 2022

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/1st_Armored_Division_trains_with_Australian_Army_%28Image_3_of_9%29.webp/250px-1st_Armored_Division_trains_with_Australian_Army_%28Image_3_of_9%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:1st_Armored_Division_trains_with_Australian_Army_(Image_3_of_9).webp)

US Army soldiers with EF88C Austeyr

The **EF88** (Enhanced F88) was developed by [Thales Australia](/wiki/Thales_Australia "Thales Australia") for the Australian Defence Force under Project LAND 125 Phase 3C to replace the F88 Austeyr.[[67]](#cite_note-EF88-67)[[68]](#cite_note-ArmyNewsFeb2012-68)[[69]](#cite_note-ADMSept2015-69) Thales Australia offers an export variant of the EF88, designated as the **F90**.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-SteyrSL40-71)

The EF88 is produced at Thales Australia's [Lithgow Arms](/wiki/Lithgow_Small_Arms_Factory "Lithgow Small Arms Factory") factory.[[72]](#cite_note-Thales2015-72) The contract to develop an improved lighter version of the F88 Austeyr was signed with Thales Australia in December 2011.[[68]](#cite_note-ArmyNewsFeb2012-68) In September 2014, Thales Australia was awarded a [low rate initial production](/wiki/Low_rate_initial_production "Low rate initial production") contract after achieving provisional design acceptance.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In June 2015, EF88s from the low rate initial production were issued to the Army infantry battalion [1 RAR](/wiki/1st_Battalion,_Royal_Australian_Regiment "1st Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment") to trial before the anticipated rollout of the EF88 in 2016.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[69]](#cite_note-ADMSept2015-69) In July 2015, the ADF placed an initial order of 30,000 rifles in two versions: a standard rifle with a 20 in (508 mm) barrel and a carbine with a 16 in (406 mm) barrel.[[72]](#cite_note-Thales2015-72)[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[67]](#cite_note-EF88-67)

In July 2020, a second order was placed for an additional 8,500 rifles.[[67]](#cite_note-EF88-67)[[76]](#cite_note-76) Internally and externally the EF88 is still similar to the Steyr AUG, although it has received many distinctive upgrades and changes.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The colour that was chosen was a black-finish compared to the two-tone colour with a "dark khaki undercarriage and a light brown upper" finish of the F88A2s.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201960-78)

Upgrades include the following:

* Longer [NATO STANAG](/wiki/NATO_Accessory_Rail "NATO Accessory Rail") top rail with a NATO STANAG bottom rail and side rail[[79]](#cite_note-ArmyPoster-79)[[80]](#cite_note-LithgowArms-80)
* A fixed lighter [fluted](/wiki/Fluting_(firearms) "Fluting (firearms)") barrel[[79]](#cite_note-ArmyPoster-79)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201959-81)
* Folding [cocking handle](/wiki/Cocking_handle "Cocking handle")[[80]](#cite_note-LithgowArms-80)[[79]](#cite_note-ArmyPoster-79)
* Extended ejection port with recessed covers[[79]](#cite_note-ArmyPoster-79)
* Enhanced buttstock profile and improved cheek weld design[[80]](#cite_note-LithgowArms-80)[[79]](#cite_note-ArmyPoster-79)
* Bolt-release catch on buttstock for faster magazine changes[[69]](#cite_note-ADMSept2015-69)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasters201958-82)
* [Elcan](/wiki/ELCAN_Optical_Technologies "ELCAN Optical Technologies") Spectre DR 1-4× enhanced day sight[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Thales tested two grenade launchers for the EF88, the Madritsch **ML40AUS** designed specifically for the EF88 and the Steyr **SL40**.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-FirearmsBlog-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87) In January 2014, Thales selected the Steyr SL40 for the EF88, and the ML40AUS reportedly had "significant" engineering concerns.[[71]](#cite_note-SteyrSL40-71)[[88]](#cite_note-Rogueadventurer-88) The ADF ordered 2,277 SL40s.[[67]](#cite_note-EF88-67) The SL40 is mounted on the EF88's bottom accessory rail and its trigger protrudes inside the rifle's trigger guard, and uses a Trijicon holographic sight for its sighting system.[[88]](#cite_note-Rogueadventurer-88)[[86]](#cite_note-FirearmsBlog-86) The SL40 is based on the Steyr GL40 grenade launcher, it weighs 1.025 kg (2.26 lb) and has a 180 mm (7.1 in) barrel length.[[86]](#cite_note-FirearmsBlog-86)[[71]](#cite_note-SteyrSL40-71) The SL40 does not require a tool to attach it or remove it from the rifle.[[69]](#cite_note-ADMSept2015-69)

Within the Australian Defence Force, there has been some discussion about the suitability of the EF88 when compared against variants of the AR-15 platform such as the [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine") and [SIG MCX](/wiki/SIG_MCX "SIG MCX").[[89]](#cite_note-89)

The Army acquired more than 500 suppressors for the EF88 that were manufactured by Oceania Defence for regular infantry battalions which were rolled out from May 2021.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In November 2021, *Defence Technology Review* reported that Thales Australia, in collaboration with the Australian Army, were developing a next-generation individual rifle in bullpup configuration chambered for the [6.8 mm calibre](/wiki/.277_Fury ".277 Fury").[[91]](#cite_note-91) In 2025, *Defence Technology Review* reported that the development of the weapon had been cancelled.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

#### F90

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: F90")]

In June 2012, Thales debuted the F90 at the [Eurosatory](/wiki/Eurosatory "Eurosatory") military exhibition in Paris. Besides the designation, the F90 is otherwise identical to the EF88.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Lithgow Arms offers the F90 in three different barrel lengths: 360 mm (14.2 in), 407 mm (16 in), and 508 mm (20 in).[[94]](#cite_note-F90-94) The barrels are fixed cold hammer forged, chrome lined and fluted,[[94]](#cite_note-F90-94) and the receiver has heat-vent cut outs for better heat ventilation.[[95]](#cite_note-95)It can also be outfitted with the SL40 grenade launcher,[[94]](#cite_note-F90-94) and its nominal cyclic rate of fire is 740 rounds per minute.[[80]](#cite_note-LithgowArms-80)

In 2015, Dasan Manufacturing was granted the rights to manufacture the F90, in an effort to bid them to the South Korean military for future replacements of the Daewoo [K2](/wiki/Daewoo_Precision_Industries_K2 "Daewoo Precision Industries K2").[[96]](#cite_note-96) It is marketed by Dasan as the DSR-90.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

At the Defexpo 2018 convention, MKU gained Indian licensing rights to manufacture the F90 for Indian contracts.[[98]](#cite_note-98) In April 2019, the F90CQB variant was planned to be submitted in conjunction with the Kalyani Group for Indian Army requirements on a 5.56 mm NATO carbine.[[99]](#cite_note-99) As of April 2020, Bharat Forge is Thales' partner to manufacture the F90.[[100]](#cite_note-100) BF will market it to Indian military and law enforcement, and for potential export sales.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

In 2016, *The Firearm Blog* reported that a semi-automatic variant of the F90, the **Atrax**, would be available to the US civilian market.[[102]](#cite_note-102) In 2018, *The Firearm Blog* reported that Dasan USA had commenced producing components for the Atrax.[[103]](#cite_note-103) In 2019, *The Firearm Blog* reported that Thales had cancelled the rifle for "ethical reasons".[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In March 2018, Thales Australia introduced the **F90MBR** (Modular Bullpup Rifle). It is a successor to the F90, being similarly marketed for export. It features a redesigned receiver, incorporating STANAG magazine compatibility[[105]](#cite_note-105) and an ambidextrous bolt catch and forward magazine release.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

### Irish adopted variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Irish adopted variants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Overseas_%2836%29_%2824489994377%29.jpg/250px-Overseas_%2836%29_%2824489994377%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Overseas_(36)_(24489994377).jpg)

Irish soldier with an AUG Mod 14 during a [UNDOF](/wiki/United_Nations_Disengagement_Observer_Force "United Nations Disengagement Observer Force") deployment

The Steyr AUG A1 entered service with the Irish Defence Forces in 1988.[[107]](#cite_note-PLW-107)

In 2014, the [Irish Army](/wiki/Irish_Army "Irish Army") began a modernisation programme to upgrade their Steyr AUG A1s, which was possible due to the modularity of the AUG. The result was the **Steyr AUG Mod 14**, and on the same year the army began issuing the rifle to its operational units.[[107]](#cite_note-PLW-107)

They replaced the original A1 housing/receiver group (with 1.5× optical sight) with an A3 housing/receiver group (with a Picatinny rail on top and right side) allowing a modern optical sight to be fitted.[[107]](#cite_note-PLW-107) The [Trijicon ACOG](/wiki/Advanced_Combat_Optical_Gunsight "Advanced Combat Optical Gunsight") 4× sight was selected as the new optical sight of the rifle.[[107]](#cite_note-PLW-107) The rifle features the ALO "automatic lockout" trigger, which can also be found in the Australian and New Zealand variants.

### New Zealand adopted variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: New Zealand adopted variants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/130416-M-OM885-092_-_New_Zealanders_maneuver%2C_engage_targets_during_Dawn_Blitz_2013.jpg/250px-130416-M-OM885-092_-_New_Zealanders_maneuver%2C_engage_targets_during_Dawn_Blitz_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:130416-M-OM885-092_-_New_Zealanders_maneuver,_engage_targets_during_Dawn_Blitz_2013.jpg)

New Zealand Army with the IW Steyr on a military scenario-driven exercise

The [New Zealand Defence Force](/wiki/New_Zealand_Defence_Force "New Zealand Defence Force") adopted the AUG A1 model, designated *Individual Weapon, Steyr* (**IW Steyr**) as its primary individual rifle. 5,000 Austrian made rifles entered service in 1988, later supplemented by a further 15,000 manufactured in Australia by ADI.[[108]](#cite_note-NZArmy-108)[[40]](#cite_note-:0-40) In 2013, *Stuff* reported that New Zealand had 13,000 IW Steyrs.[[109]](#cite_note-109) In 2015, New Zealand selected the Lewis Machine and Tool [Mars-L 5.56mm rifle](/wiki/Lewis_Machine_and_Tool_Company#MARS-L "Lewis Machine and Tool Company") to replace the IW Steyr and began transitioning to the new rifle in 2017.[[110]](#cite_note-stuffnz-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)

The variants of the New Zealand IW Steyr were equipped with a progressive trigger (without full-auto lock-out tab as seen on F88 models) and a three-position safety. The sight added a crosshair to the circle reticule. New Zealand issued both factory and locally modified carbines alongside the full-length rifle variant.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Civilian variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Civilian variants")]

* The **Steyr AUG P** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG A1 available to the civilian and law enforcement markets. It features the carbine length, 407 mm (16 in) barrel and a modified bolt, carrier and trigger assembly that will only allow semi-automatic fire. The AUG P also has a slightly different optical sight that features a reticule with a fine dot in the centre of the aiming circle, allowing for more precise aiming.
* The **Steyr AUG P Special Receiver** is similar to the AUG P but features a STANAG scope mount system on top of the receiver.
* The **Steyr AUG SA** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG A1, built for civilian use and import to the US before being banned from importation in 1989.
* The **Steyr AUG Z** is a semi-automatic only variant in compliance with Austrian weapon laws, somewhat similar to the AUG A2 but lacking the quick detachable barrels and is unable to accept the trigger group from the assault rifles. It is intended primarily for civilian use.[[112]](#cite_note-112)
* The **Steyr AUG Z Sport** is a semi-automatic only variant, somewhat similar to the AUG Z, for use in sport shooting approved by the BKA in Germany. This variant has a special handguard without the typical front grip.[[113]](#cite_note-113)
* The **Steyr AUG Z SP** was a straight pull only configuration, somewhat similar to the AUG Z, and was intended primarily for civilian use; it was sold only in the United Kingdom.[[114]](#cite_note-114)
* The **Steyr AUG Z A3** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG Z similar to the AUG A3 introduced in 2010.
* The **Steyr AUG Z A3 9mm** is a semi-automatic only 9×19mm Parabellum variant of the AUG Z A3.
* The **Steyr AUG Z A3 SE** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG Z similar to the AUG A3 SF.
* The **Steyr USR** is an AUG A2 modified to meet the former Federal Assault Weapons Ban (AWB) (or Public Safety and Recreational Firearms Use Protection Act) regulations. The USR has a thumb hole stock molded in gray polymer. The barrel is made by GSI Inc and is a 20” bull barrel. No threads so no muzzle device. The barrel takedown button has been cut off.
* The **Steyr AUG A3 SA USA** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG A3 with a 407 mm (16.0 in) barrel, made available for the U.S. civilian market in April 2009.[[115]](#cite_note-USA-115)
* The **Steyr AUG A3 SA NATO:** is a semi-automatic only variant similar to the AUG A3 SA USA, but uses a right-hand-only, [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") [STANAG magazine](/wiki/STANAG_magazine "STANAG magazine") stock assembly.[[19]](#cite_note-steyr-aug.com-19)[[20]](#cite_note-auto-20)
* The **Steyr AUG A3 M1** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG A3 SF with a detachable optical sight which can be replaced with [Picatinny rails](/wiki/Picatinny_rail "Picatinny rail") and a 416 mm (16.4 in) barrel length, manufactured in the US by Steyr Arms US since October 2014.[[116]](#cite_note-116)
* The **Steyr AUG A3 M2** is a semi-automatic only variant of the AUG A3 with an extended Picatinny rail and a 416 mm (16.4 in) barrel length. It reverted to the A1 style charging handle with a push-button forward assist and an added slot to lock it in the forward position. It was designed to accommodate an [M-LOK](/wiki/M-LOK "M-LOK") handguard manufactured by Steyr. It has been manufactured in the US by Steyr Arms US since April 2024.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

### AUG clones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: AUG clones")]

* The **[STG-556](/wiki/STG-556 "STG-556")** was introduced at the 2007 [SHOT Show](/wiki/SHOT_Show "SHOT Show"), it was manufactured by [Microtech Small Arms Research](/wiki/Microtech_Small_Arms_Research "Microtech Small Arms Research") Inc. (a subsidiary of [Microtech Knives](/wiki/Microtech_Knives "Microtech Knives")) an AUG A1 clone significantly re-engineered in its working system and principle as it features a bolt hold-open device as seen on the M16 rifle; otherwise the MSAR STG-556 retains the original AUG features, such as feeding from proprietary translucent plastic magazines and having the quick-change barrel option. The STG-556 can be converted from either having a telescopic sight or a Picatinny rail. It is available in either civilian (semi-automatic only) configuration, and military and law enforcement (selective fire) configuration.[[12]](#cite_note-Choat-12)[[118]](#cite_note-118)
* The **AXR** was revealed at the 2007 SHOT Show, manufactured by Tactical Products Design Inc. as an AUG A2 clone capable of semi-automatic only fire, aimed for both the civilian and law enforcement markets, and fed by STANAG magazines; the manufacturer sells clear plastic magazines which are STANAG 4179 compliant and will readily fit in any rifle with a compatible magazine catch.[[119]](#cite_note-119) The rifle does not have the integral scope, allowing users to use any kind of scopes or laser sights on the Picatinny rail.[[120]](#cite_note-120)
* The **Oberland Arms OA-UG** is a German clone of the AUG intended for use in Germany in civilian sales.[[121]](#cite_note-121)
* The **Type 68**[[122]](#cite_note-jones2009-122)[[123]](#cite_note-kemp2009-123) is a Taiwanese copy of the AUG with notable differences including a smaller trigger guard and the use of iron sights instead of the original's telescopic sight (although optical sights can still be optionally mounted on the carrying handle). Developed as a potential alternative to the T65 assault rifle and (in the form of a heavy-barrel variant) replacement to the [Type 57A](/wiki/M14_rifle#International_service "M14 rifle") assault rifle, it ultimately did not enter service after the ROC military decided to adopt the [Minimi](/wiki/FN_Minimi "FN Minimi") and [T75](/wiki/T75_Light_machine_gun "T75 Light machine gun") as their future light machine gun.[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steyr_AUG&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Military")]

* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Flag_of_Algeria.svg/40px-Flag_of_Algeria.svg.png) [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"): [Special Intervention Detachment](/wiki/Special_Intervention_Detachment "Special Intervention Detachment")[[136]](#cite_note-136)
* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Flag_of_Argentina.svg/40px-Flag_of_Argentina.svg.png) [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"): [Argentine Armed Forces](/wiki/Argentine_Armed_Forces "Argentine Armed Forces").[[137]](#cite_note-137)
